title: PhD Presentations
text:
Departure from Normality
A contaminated normal distribution given by Tukey and Huber can be represented as, \[F=(1-\epsilon) G+\epsilon H\] where, \(G = N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) and H is any distribution.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Location Model
\(x_i = \mu + u_i\) where \(u_i\) \((i=1, \dots, n)\) are random variables. \[
\begin{align*}
    F(x) &= P(x_i \leq x)        && \text{Definition of CDF} & \\
         &= P(\mu + u_i \leq x)  && \text{Substitute model } x_i = \mu + u_i & \\
         &= P(u_i \leq x - \mu)  && \text{Rearrange to isolate } u_i & \\
         &= F_0(x - \mu)         && \text{Apply error distribution } F_0 & \\
    \therefore F(x) &= F_0(x - \mu) && &
\end{align*}
\]
Here, \(x_i\) are i.i.d. random variables.
Also, \(F_0(x) = 1 - F_0(-x)\).
If \(F_0 = N(0, \sigma^2)\), then,

\(\text{mean}(x) \sim N(0,\, \sigma^2/n)\)
\(\text{med}(x) \approx N(0,\, 1.57 \times \sigma^2/n)\)

Thus, for a pure normal distribution, mean is 57% more efficient than the median.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Formulation
Consider \(f_0 = F_0'\), the density of the location model. Then, the joint density of the model is given as,
\[L(x_i;\, \mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_0(x_i - \mu)\]
The MLE of \(\mu\) is \(\hat{\mu}\), such that,
\[\hat{\mu} = \hat{\mu}(x_i) = \arg\max_{\mu}\, L(x_i;\, \mu)\]
If \(F_0\) is exactly known, the MLE will be the optimal. Since \(F_0\) is only approximately known, the goal is to find “nearly optimal” estimators for,

when \(F_0\) is normal
when \(F_0\) is approximately normal

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Estimation
If \(f_0\) is everywhere positive, since the logarithm is an increasing function, \(\hat{\mu}\) can be written as,
\[\hat{\mu} = \arg\min_{\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho(x_i - \mu)\]
where,
\[\rho = -\log f_0\]
For some unknown \(f_0\) and a function \(\rho\), this problem can be solved using the implicit equation,
\[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \psi(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = 0\]
where,
\[\psi = \rho'\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Weighted Mean Interpretation
The equation,
\[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \psi(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = 0\]
can be written as,
\[\sum_{i=1}^{n} W(x_i - \hat{\mu})(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = 0\]
\[\implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \hat{\mu}\]
\[\implies \hat{\mu} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}\]






Weighted Mean of Sample


\(\hat{\mu} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}\) is simply a weighted mean of the sample.



where,
\[W(x) = \begin{cases} \psi(x)/x & \text{if } x \neq 0 \\ \psi'(0) & \text{if } x = 0 \end{cases}\]
and,
\[W_i = W(x_i - \hat{\mu})\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Formal Definition
A \(\rho\)-function will denote a function \(\rho\) such that:

\(\rho(x)\) is a nondecreasing function of \(|x|\)
\(\rho(0) = 0\)
\(\rho(x)\) is increasing for \(x > 0\) such that \(\rho(x) < \rho(\infty)\)
if \(\rho\) is bounded, it is also assumed that \(\rho(\infty) = 1\).

A \(\psi\)-function will denote a function \(\psi\) that is the derivative of a \(\rho\)-function, which implies in particular that

\(\psi\) is odd and \(\psi(x) \geq 0\) for \(x \geq 0\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Yet another interpretation
Another intuitive way to interpret an M-estimator is, \[
\hat{\mu} = \hat{\mu} + \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \psi(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \zeta(x_i, \hat{\mu})
\] where \[
\zeta(x, \mu) = \mu + \psi(x - \mu)
\] In other words, \(\hat{\mu}\) may be viewed as an average of modified observations \(\zeta(x_i, \hat{\mu})\), also called pseudo observations:observations in the bulk of the data remain unchanged, while those too large or too small are truncated.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Common M-Estimator Functions









Estimator
Objective Function \(\rho(x)\)
Influence Function \(\psi(x)\)
Weight Function \(W(x)\)




Huber
\(\begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}x^2 & |x| \leq k \\ k|x| - \frac{1}{2}k^2 & |x| > k \end{cases}\)
\(\begin{cases} x & |x| \leq k \\ k\,\text{sgn}(x) & |x| > k \end{cases}\)
\(\min\left(1,\, \dfrac{k}{|x|}\right)\)


Bisquare
\(\begin{cases} \frac{k^2}{6}\left(1 - \left[1-\left(\frac{x}{k}\right)^2\right]^3\right) & |x| \leq k \\ \frac{k^2}{6} & |x| > k \end{cases}\)
\(\begin{cases} x\left[1-\left(\frac{x}{k}\right)^2\right]^2 & |x| \leq k \\ 0 & |x| > k \end{cases}\)
\(\begin{cases} \left[1-\left(\frac{x}{k}\right)^2\right]^2 & |x| \leq k \\ 0 & |x| > k \end{cases}\)


Hampel
\(\frac{1}{C}\begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}x^2 & |x| \leq a \\ \frac{1}{2}a^2 + a(|x|-a) & a < |x| \leq b \\ \frac{a}{2}\left(2b - a + (|x|-b)\left[1+\frac{r-|x|}{r-b}\right]\right) & b < |x| \leq r \\ C & r < |x| \end{cases}\)
\(\begin{cases} x & |x| \leq a \\ a\,\text{sgn}(x) & a < |x| \leq b \\ a\,\text{sgn}(x)\dfrac{r-|x|}{r-b} & b < |x| \leq r \\ 0 & r < |x| \end{cases}\)
\(\begin{cases} 1 & |x| \leq a \\ \frac{a}{|x|} & a < |x| \leq b \\ \frac{a}{|x|}\dfrac{r-|x|}{r-b} & b < |x| \leq r \\ 0 & r < |x| \end{cases}\)


Welsh
\(\dfrac{k^2}{2}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{k}\right)^2\right)\right]\)
\(x\exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{k}\right)^2\right)\)
\(\exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{k}\right)^2\right)\)



* Note for Hampel: \(C = \frac{a}{2}(b - a + r)\).

title: PhD Presentations
text: Behaviour of Common M-Estimators

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Definition
The \(\alpha\)-trimmed mean is defined as:
\[
\bar{x}_\alpha = \frac{1}{n - 2m} \sum_{i=m+1}^{n-m} x_{(i)}
\]
The \(\alpha\)-Winsorized mean is defined as:
\[
\tilde{x}_\alpha = \frac{1}{n} \left( m x_{(m+1)} + m x_{(n-m)} + \sum_{i=m+1}^{n-m} x_{(i)} \right)
\]
where,

\(n\): The total number of observations.
\(x_{(i)}\): The order statistics of the sample, such that \(x_{(1)} \leq x_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq x_{(n)}\).
\(\alpha\): The trimming proportion, where \(\alpha \in [0, 1/2)\).
\(m\): The integer number of trimmed values, defined as \(m = [n\alpha]\), where \([\cdot]\) stands for the integer part.







Limiting Case



Case \(\alpha \to 0\) (\(m = 0\)): No data is discarded. Both estimators reduce to the standard Sample Mean.
Case \(\alpha \to 0.5\) (\(m \approx n/2\)): The maximum amount of data is trimmed/replaced. Both estimators converge to the Sample Median.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Definition
A more general class of estimators, called L-estimators, is defined as linear combinations of order statistics:
\[
\hat{\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_{(i)},
\]
where the \(a_i\)s are given constants. For \(\alpha\)-trimmed means,
\[
a_i = \frac{1}{n - 2m} \text{I}(m + 1 \leq i \leq n - m),
\]
and for \(\alpha\)-Winsorized means
\[
a_i = \frac{1}{n} \Big( (m + 1)\text{I}(i = m + 1) + (m + 1)\text{I}(i = n - m) + \text{I}(m + 1 < i < n - m) \Big) .
\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Equivariance
If the coefficients of an L-estimator satisfy the conditions
\[
a_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i = 1, \quad a_i = a_{n-i+1},
\]
then the estimator is shift and scale equivariant, and also fulfills the natural conditions

If \(x_i \geq 0\) for all \(i\), then \(\hat{\mu} \geq 0\)
If \(x_i = c\) for all \(i\), then \(\hat{\mu} = c\)
\(\hat{\mu}(-x) = -\hat{\mu}(x)\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Scale Model
\(x_i = \sigma u_i\) where \(u_i \ (i = 1,\dots,n)\) are i.i.d. random variables with density \(f_0\) and \(\sigma > 0\).
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(x) &= P(x_i \leq x) & &\text{Definition of CDF} \\
&= P(\sigma u_i \leq x) & &\text{Substitute model } x_i = \sigma u_i \\
&= P\left(u_i \leq \frac{x}{\sigma}\right) & &\text{Rearrange to isolate } u_i \\
&= F_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) & &\text{Apply error distribution } F_0
\end{aligned}
\]
\(\therefore F(x) = F_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)\)
Differentiating the CDF with respect to \(x\) yields the density (PDF):
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= \frac{d}{dx} F(x) & &\text{Definition of PDF} \\
&= \frac{d}{dx} F_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) & &\text{Substitute derived CDF} \\
&= f_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma} & &\text{Apply Chain Rule}
\end{aligned}
\]
\(\therefore f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma} f_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)\)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Estimation
The MLE of \(\sigma\) is found from
\[
\hat{\sigma} = \arg\max_{\sigma} \frac{1}{\sigma^n} \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_0 \left( \frac{x_i}{\sigma} \right)
\]
Taking logs and differentiating with respect to \(\sigma\), yields the condition:
\[
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho \left( \frac{x_i}{\sigma} \right) = 1
\]
where \(\rho(t) = t\psi(t)\) and \(\psi = -f'_0/f_0\). Generalizing this result, any estimator \(\hat{\sigma}\) satisfying the equation
\[
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho \left( \frac{x_i}{\hat{\sigma}} \right) = \delta
\]
where \(\delta\) is a positive constant, is called an M-estimator of scale.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Dispersion Estimators

Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) It measures the median distance of observations from the sample median.
\[
\text{MAD} = \text{median}(|x_i - \text{median}(x)|)
\]
Mean Absolute Deviation (MD) The arithmetic mean of the absolute differences between observations and the sample mean.
\[
\text{MD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - \bar{x}|
\]
Interquartile Range (IQR) The difference between the 75th and 25th percentiles.
\[
\text{IQR} = x_{(n-m+1)} - x_{(m)}
\]
where \(m \approx n/4\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Multiples of Scale
If \(x \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), then \(\text{SD}(x) = \sigma\). The other dispersion estimators are constant multiples of \(\sigma\):
\[
\text{MD}(x) = c_1 \sigma \qquad \text{MAD}(x) = c_2 \sigma \qquad \text{IQR}(x) = 2c_2 \sigma
\]
Where the approximate constant values are:
\[
c_1 = 2\varphi(0) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \approx 0.7979 \qquad c_2 = \Phi^{-1}(0.75) \approx 0.675
\]
To create a dispersion estimator that “measures the same thing” as the standard deviation for the normal distribution, we define the Normalized MAD (MADN):
\[
\text{MADN}(x) = \frac{\text{MAD}(x)}{0.675}
\]
Likewise, we can normalize the MD and the IQR by dividing them by \(c_1\) and by \(2c_2\) respectively.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
M-Dispersion Estimators
An estimator that combines the robustness of MADN and the efficiency of SD and MD is the following:

first compute a location M-estimator \(\hat{\mu}\).
Then apply a scale M-estimator \(\hat{\sigma}\) to the centered data \(x_i - \hat{\mu}\).
Here \(\hat{\sigma}\) should be normalized as described. This \(\hat{\sigma}\) can be called the M-dispersion estimator.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The \(Q_n\) alternative
Unlike the SD, MD, and MAD, an estimator called the \(Q_n\) estimator (Croux and Rousseeuw, 1992) does not require centering the data.

It is based strictly on the differences between data values: \(d_{ij} = |x_i - x_j|\) for \(i < j\).
Let \(m = \binom{n}{2}\) be the number of differences, ordered as \(d_{(1)} \leq \dots \leq d_{(m)}\).
Define \[
h = \lfloor \frac{n}{2} \rfloor + 1, \qquad k = \binom{h}{2}.
\] The estimator is defined as the \(k\)-th order statistic: \(Q_n = d_{(k)}\).
Since \(k \approx m/4\), \(Q_n\) is approximately the first quartile of the \(d_{ij}\) values.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Location M-Estimators

Initialization: Start with a robust dispersion estimator (\(\hat{\sigma}\)) and an initial location estimator (\(\hat{\mu}_0\)): \[\hat{\sigma} = \text{MADN}(\mathbf{x}), \quad \hat{\mu}_0 = \text{Med}(\mathbf{x})\]
Iterative Procedure (\(k = 0, 1, 2, \dots\)):

Compute the weights: \[w_{k,i} = W \left( \frac{x_i - \hat{\mu}_k}{\hat{\sigma}} \right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n\]
Update the location estimate: \[\hat{\mu}_{k+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{k,i} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{k,i}}\]

Stopping Rule: Halt when the change falls below a tolerance parameter \(\varepsilon\): \[|\hat{\mu}_{k+1} - \hat{\mu}_k| < \varepsilon \hat{\sigma}\]
Convergence Properties:

If \(\psi\) is increasing: The solution is unique.
If \(\psi\) is redescending: Choosing a robust \(\hat{\mu}_0\) ensures convergence to a valid solution.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Scale M-Estimators

Initialization: Start with an initial robust dispersion estimator (\(\hat{\sigma}_0\)): \[\hat{\sigma}_0 = \text{MADN}(\mathbf{x})\]
Iterative Procedure (\(k = 0, 1, 2, \dots\)):

Compute the weights: \[w_{k,i} = W \left( \frac{x_i}{\hat{\sigma}_k} \right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n\]
Update the scale estimate: \[\hat{\sigma}_{k+1} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n\delta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{k,i} x_i^2 }\]

Stopping Rule: Halt when the relative change falls below a tolerance parameter \(\varepsilon\): \[\left| \frac{\hat{\sigma}_{k+1}}{\hat{\sigma}_k} - 1 \right| < \varepsilon\]
Convergence Properties:

The sequence \(\hat{\sigma}_k\) converges to a valid solution if \(W(x)\) is:

bounded
even
continuous
nonincreasing for \(x > 0\)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Simultaneous M-estimators

Initialization: Compute robust starting values for both location and dispersion: \(\hat{\mu}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0\)
Iterative Procedure (\(k = 0, 1, 2, \dots\)):

Compute standardized residuals: \[r_{k,i} = \frac{x_i - \hat{\mu}_k}{\hat{\sigma}_k} \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n\]
Compute dual weights (\(W_1\) for location, \(W_2\) for scale): \[w_{1k,i} = W_1(r_{k,i}), \quad w_{2k,i} = W_2(r_{k,i})\]
Update estimates simultaneously: \[\hat{\mu}_{k+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{1k,i} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{1k,i}}\] \[\hat{\sigma}_{k+1}^2 = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_k^2}{n\delta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{2k,i} r_{k,i}^2\]

Stopping Rule: Halt when both estimates stabilize below a tolerance parameter \(\varepsilon\): \[|\hat{\mu}_{k+1} - \hat{\mu}_k| < \varepsilon \hat{\sigma}_k \quad \text{and} \quad \left| \frac{\hat{\sigma}_{k+1}}{\hat{\sigma}_k} - 1 \right| < \varepsilon\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Location Estimators

library(robustbase)

data("telef")
x <- telef$Calls   # telephone calls — contains high-leverage outliers

# ----- Huber M-estimator of Location -----
# huberM(x, k, tol, mu, s, se)
#   x  : numeric vector
#   k  : tuning constant — boundary between quadratic and linear loss
#        default k = 1.5; use k = 1.345 for 95% efficiency at the Normal
#   mu : initial location estimate, default = median(x)
#   s  : initial scale estimate,    default = mad(x)
#   se : logical; compute standard error? default FALSE
huberM(x, k = 1.5)

$mu
[1] 2.21057

$s
[1] 1.512252

$it
[1] 11

$SE
[1] NA

# ----- Trimmed Mean -----
# mean(x, trim)
#   trim : fraction (0 to 0.5) of observations dropped
#          from each tail before computing the mean
mean(x, trim = 0.10)     # removes bottom 10% and top 10%

[1] 3.9835

# ----- Winsorized Mean (base R) -----
# Replaces the bottom and top `trim` fraction of values
# with the nearest kept boundary value, then takes the mean
winsor_mean <- function(x, trim = 0.10) {
  x_s <- sort(x)
  m   <- floor(trim * length(x_s))
  x_s[seq_len(m)]                              <- x_s[m + 1]
  x_s[seq(length(x_s) - m + 1, length(x_s))] <- x_s[length(x_s) - m]
  mean(x_s)
}

winsor_mean(x, trim = 0.10)

[1] 4.68375

# ----- Summary -----
cat(
  "Mean           :", round(mean(x),               4), "\n",
  "Huber (k = 1.5)  :", round(huberM(x, k = 1.5)$mu, 4), "\n",
  "Trimmed  (10%)   :", round(mean(x, trim = 0.10),   4), "\n",
  "Winsorized (10%) :", round(winsor_mean(x, 0.10),   4), "\n",
  sep = ""
)

Mean           :4.9992
Huber (k = 1.5)  :2.2106
Trimmed  (10%)   :3.9835
Winsorized (10%) :4.6838

library(robustbase)

data("telef")

# ----- Classical -----
sd(x)                    # Standard deviation

[1] 6.553211

# ----- MAD and MADN -----
# mad(x, center, constant)
#   center   : central value, default = median(x)
#   constant : 1      -> raw MAD
#              1.4826 -> MADN, consistent estimator of SD at Normal (default)
mad(x, constant = 1)     # Raw MAD

[1] 1.02

mad(x)                   # MADN

[1] 1.512252

IQR(x) / 1.349           # Divide by 1.349 to make consistent with SD at Normal

[1] 4.010378

# ----- scaleTau2 -----
# scaleTau2(x, c1, c2, consistency, mu.too, na.rm)
#   c1          : tuning constant for location weights (default 4.5)
#   c2          : tuning constant for scale rho function (default 3.0)
#   consistency : divide by asymptotic limit for consistency at Normal (default TRUE)
#   mu.too      : if TRUE, returns named vector c(mu, sigma) instead of just sigma
#   na.rm       : remove NAs before computing (default FALSE)
scaleTau2(x, mu.too = TRUE)

[1] 1.357990 1.841955

# Qn(x, constant, na.rm)
#   k-th order statistic of all pairwise |x_i - x_j|; no centering needed
#   constant : consistency factor; default gives consistency at Normal
#   na.rm    : remove NAs (default FALSE)
Qn(x)

# Sn(x, constant, finite.corr, na.rm)
#   median_i { median_j |x_i - x_j| }; no centering needed
#   constant    : consistency factor; default gives consistency at Normal
#   finite.corr : apply finite-sample bias correction (default TRUE)
#   na.rm       : remove NAs (default FALSE)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Pseudo Observations via huberize()

library(robustbase)

data("telef")
x <- telef$Calls

# Step 1: obtain robust location via huberM()
fit <- huberM(x, k = 1.5)
cat("Huber mu :", round(fit$mu, 4), "\n")

Huber mu : 2.2106 

cat("Huber s  :", round(fit$s,  4), "\n\n")

Huber s  : 1.5123 

# Step 2: huberize(x, M, c, k)
#   x : numeric vector of observations
#   M : location center — default = huberM(x, k = k)$mu
#   c : half-width multiplier — boundary = [M - c*Qn(x), M + c*Qn(x)]
#       observations outside this interval are pulled to the boundary
#       default c = k = 1.5
#   k : tuning constant used when computing default M via huberM()
# NOTE: scale is computed INTERNALLY as Qn(x); it is NOT a user argument
x_pseudo <- huberize(x, M = fit$mu, c = 1.5, k = 1.5)

# Step 3: identify modified (outlying) observations
modified <- which(round(x, 8) != round(x_pseudo, 8))

cat(
  "Total observations   :", length(x), "\n",
  "Unchanged (bulk)     :", length(x) - length(modified), "\n",
  "Modified (truncated) :", length(modified), "\n\n")

Total observations   : 24

# Step 4: table of original vs pseudo for modified observations
data.frame(
  Year     = 1900L + as.integer(telef$Year[modified]),
  Original = round(x[modified],        4),

title: Contributing to MathJax
text:
You are interested in giving us a hand? That’s awesome! We’ve put together some brief guidelines that should help you get started quickly and easily.
There are lots and lots of ways to get involved, this document covers:

reporting an issue

bug reports
feature requests
change requests

working on MathJax core

key branches and tags
submitting pull requests
testing and quality assurance
writing documentation
translation

Conduct



If you’re about to raise an issue because you think you’ve found a problem with MathJax, or you’d like to make a request for a new feature in the codebase, or any other reason, please read this first.
The MathJax issue tracker is the preferred channel for bug reports, feature requests, change requests, and submitting pull requests, but please respect the following restrictions:

Please search for existing issues. Help us keep duplicate issues to a minimum by checking to see if someone has already reported your problem or requested your idea.
Please do not use the issue tracker for personal support requests (use the MathJax User Group).
Please be civil. Keep the discussion on topic and respect the opinions of others. See also our Conduct Guidelines



A bug is a demonstrable problem that is caused by the code in the repository. Good bug reports are extremely helpful — thank you!
Guidelines for bug reports:

Use the GitHub issue search — check if the issue has already been reported.
Check if the issue has been fixed — look for closed issues in the current milestone or try to reproduce it using the latest develop branch. Please note that you will need to compile MathJax and make the components in order to test MathJax from the source repository.
Share a live sample of the problem — without a live page it is usually impossible to debug problems; see also the Bug Report Template below.
Isolate the problem — a live sample is a starting point but if you want to speed things up, create a reduced test case. Be specific about your setup (browser, OS versions, etc). Use services like jsbin, CodePen, or jsFiddle to make collaboration on minimal test cases easier for everyone.
Include a screenshot/cast as a last resort — Is your issue about a layout or design feature or bug that is hard to reproduce or isolate? Then please provide a screenshot or screencast. Tools like LICEcap or SauceLabs allow you to quickly and easily record a screencasts. If you make it an animated gif, you can embed it directly into your GitHub issue.
Use the Bug Report Template below or click this link to start creating a bug report with the template automatically.

A good bug report shouldn’t leave others needing to request more information from you. Be sure to include the details of your environment.

Template Example (click to use):
Short and descriptive example bug report title

### Issue Summary

A summary of the issue and the browser/OS environment in which it occurs. If
suitable, include the steps required to reproduce the bug.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. This is the first step
2. This is the second step
3. Further steps, etc.

Any other information you want to share that is relevant to the issue
being reported. Especially, why do you consider this to be a bug? What
do you expect to happen instead?

### Technical details:

* MathJax Version: 2.3 (latest commit: f3aaf3a2a3e964df2770dc4aaaa9c87ce5f47e2c)
* Client OS: Mac OS X 10.8.4
* Browser: Chrome 29.0.1547.57



Feature requests are welcome. Before you submit one, be sure to have:

Used the GitHub search to check that the feature hasn’t already been requested.
Take a moment to think about whether your idea fits with the scope and aims of the project, or if it might better fit being a custom extension.
Remember, it’s up to you to make a strong case to convince the project’s leaders of the merits of this feature. Please provide as much detail and context as possible, this means explaining the use case and why it is likely to be common.




Change requests cover both architectural and functional changes to how MathJax works. If you have an idea for a new or different dependency, a refactor, or an improvement to a feature, etc., please be sure to:

Use the GitHub search to check that someone else didn’t get there first.
Take a moment to think about the best way to make a case for, and explain what you’re thinking. Are you sure this shouldn’t really be a bug report or a feature request? Is it really one idea or is it many? What’s the context? What problem are you solving? Why is what you are suggesting better than what’s already there?





You want to contribute code? We describe how below. First, note that the MathJax source code is in the https://github.com/mathjax/MathJax-src repository, not the https://github.com/mathjax/MathJax repository, which contains the packaged component files for distribution on CDNs and the mathjax npm package (the source code is included in the mathjax-full npm package).


MathJax uses several permanent branches in the MathJax source repository:

develop is the development branch. All work on the next release happens here so you should generally branch off develop if you are going to submit a pull request. Do NOT use this branch for a production site.
master contains the latest release of MathJax. This branch may be used in production. Do NOT use this branch to work on MathJax’s source.

These branches reflect version 3 of MathJax, which is substantially different from the version 2 codebase. Version 2 will continue to be maintained while web sites transition to version 3, with work being done using the following branches in the MathJax distribution repository:

legacy-v2-develop is the development branch for changes to the legacy version 2 code. Any pull requests for version 2 should be branched from here. Do NOT use this branch for a production site.
legacy-v2 is the branch that contains any updates to version 2 following the release of version 3. Do NOT use this branch to work on MathJax’s source.

In addition to these branches, MathJax uses tags to identify the various versions. These can be checked out to obtain the specified release; for example, git checkout 2.7.5 would get you the files for version 2.7.5 of MathJax.
Note that version 3 is written in Typescript, and so must be compiled to obtain usable javascript files, and that the components need to be built once that is done. See the documentation for details. For version 2, the source javascript files are not compressed until a release is made, so you should use the copies in the unpacked directory during development.



Pull requests are welcome. If you’re looking to submit a PR for something that doesn’t have an open issue, please consider raising an issue that your PR can close, especially if you’re fixing a bug. This makes it more likely that there will be enough information available for your PR to be properly tested and merged.


If you’re not completely clear on how to submit/update/do Pull Requests, please check out our source control policies. For more insights, check the excellent in depth Git Workflow guide from Ghost, in particular

Ghost Workflow guide: commit messages





If you’re looking to get involved with the code base and don’t know where to start, checking out and testing a pull request is one of the most useful things you could do.
These are some excellent instructions on configuring your GitHub repository to allow you to checkout pull requests in the same way as branches.



MathJax’s main documentation can be found at docs.mathjax.org. The source of the docs is hosted in the mathjax/MathJax-docs repo here on GitHub.
The documentation is generated using Sphinx-doc and hosted on Read the docs. You can clone the repo and submit pull requests following the pull-request guidelines.



If you wish to add or update translations of MathJax, please do it on TranslateWiki.net (and while you’re there you can help other open source projects, too).
For bug reports and other questions that don’t fit on TranslateWiki.net, head over to the mathjax/mathjax-i18n repository.
The translation files currently are for version 2, as localization hasn’t been added to version 3 yet.




As a NumFOCUS fiscally sponsored project, MathJax is governed by the NumFOCUS code of conduct, which we summarize as follows:
We are committed to providing a friendly, safe and welcoming environment for all, regardless of gender, sexual orientation, disability, ethnicity, religion, or similar personal characteristic.
Please be kind and courteous. There’s no need to be mean or rude. Respect that people have differences of opinion and that every design or implementation choice carries a trade-off and numerous costs. There is seldom a right answer, merely an optimal answer given a set of values and circumstances.
Please keep unstructured critique to a minimum. If you have solid ideas you want to experiment with, make a fork and see how it works.
We will exclude you from interaction if you insult, demean or harass anyone. That is not welcome behaviour. We interpret the term “harassment” as including the definition in the Unacceptable Behavior section of the NumFOCUS code of conduct; if you have any lack of clarity about what might be included in that concept, please read that definition. In particular, we don’t tolerate behavior that excludes people in socially marginalized groups.
Private harassment is also unacceptable. No matter who you are, if you feel you have been or are being harassed or made uncomfortable by a community member, please contact one of the channel ops or any of the MathJax core team immediately. Whether you’re a regular contributor or a newcomer, we care about making this community a safe place for you and we’ve got your back.
Likewise any spamming, trolling, flaming, baiting, or other attention-stealing behaviour is not welcome.
We also recommend that you read discourse’s rules for further suggestions on appropriate behavior.




We heavily borrowed from Mozilla and Ghost – thank you!

https://github.com/TryGhost/Ghost/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md
https://github.com/mozilla/rust/wiki/Note-development-policy

https://github.com/jden/CONTRIBUTING.md/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md
http://blog.discourse.org/2013/03/the-universal-rules-of-civilized-discourse/

First, note that the MathJax source code is in the https://github.com/mathjax/MathJax-src repository, not the https://github.com/mathjax/MathJax repository, which contains the packaged component files for distribution on CDNs and the mathjax npm package (the source code is included in the mathjax-full npm package).


MathJax uses several permanent branches in the MathJax source repository:

develop is the development branch. All work on the next release happens here so you should generally branch off develop if you are going to submit a pull request. Do NOT use this branch for a production site.
master contains the latest release of MathJax. This branch may be used in production. Do NOT use this branch to work on MathJax’s source.

These branches reflect version 3 of MathJax, which is substantially different from the version 2 codebase. Version 2 will continue to be maintained while web sites transition to version 3, with work being done using the following branches in the MathJax distribution repository:

legacy-v2-develop is the development branch for changes to the legacy version 2 code. Any pull requests for version 2 should be branched from here. Do NOT use this branch for a production site.
legacy-v2 is the branch that contains any updates to version 2 following the release of version 3. Do NOT use this branch to work on MathJax’s source.

In addition to these branches, MathJax uses tags to identify the various versions. These can be checked out to obtain the specified release; for example, git checkout 2.7.5 would get you the files for version 2.7.5 of MathJax.
Note that version 3 is written in Typescript, and so must be compiled to obtain usable javascript files, and that the components need to be built once that is done. See the documentation for details. For version 2, the source javascript files are not compressed until a release is made, so you should use the copies in the unpacked directory during development.



Pull requests are welcome. If you’re looking to submit a PR for something that doesn’t have an open issue, please consider raising an issue that your PR can close, especially if you’re fixing a bug. This makes it more likely that there will be enough information available for your PR to be properly tested and merged.


If you’re not completely clear on how to submit/update/do Pull Requests, please check out our source control policies. For more insights, check the excellent in depth Git Workflow guide from Ghost, in particular

Ghost Workflow guide: commit messages





If you’re looking to get involved with the code base and don’t know where to start, checking out and testing a pull request is one of the most useful things you could do.
These are some excellent instructions on configuring your GitHub repository to allow you to checkout pull requests in the same way as branches.



MathJax’s main documentation can be found at docs.mathjax.org. The source of the docs is hosted in the mathjax/MathJax-docs repo here on GitHub.
The documentation is generated using Sphinx-doc and hosted on Read the docs. You can clone the repo and submit pull requests following the pull-request guidelines.



If you wish to add or update translations of MathJax, please do it on TranslateWiki.net (and while you’re there you can help other open source projects, too).
For bug reports and other questions that don’t fit on TranslateWiki.net, head over to the mathjax/mathjax-i18n repository.
The translation files currently are for version 2, as localization hasn’t been added to version 3 yet.

We are committed to providing a friendly, safe and welcoming environment for all, regardless of gender, sexual orientation, disability, ethnicity, religion, or similar personal characteristic.
Please be kind and courteous. There’s no need to be mean or rude. Respect that people have differences of opinion and that every design or implementation choice carries a trade-off and numerous costs. There is seldom a right answer, merely an optimal answer given a set of values and circumstances.
Please keep unstructured critique to a minimum. If you have solid ideas you want to experiment with, make a fork and see how it works.
We will exclude you from interaction if you insult, demean or harass anyone. That is not welcome behaviour. We interpret the term “harassment” as including the definition in the Unacceptable Behavior section of the NumFOCUS code of conduct; if you have any lack of clarity about what might be included in that concept, please read that definition. In particular, we don’t tolerate behavior that excludes people in socially marginalized groups.
Private harassment is also unacceptable. No matter who you are, if you feel you have been or are being harassed or made uncomfortable by a community member, please contact one of the channel ops or any of the MathJax core team immediately. Whether you’re a regular contributor or a newcomer, we care about making this community a safe place for you and we’ve got your back.
Likewise any spamming, trolling, flaming, baiting, or other attention-stealing behaviour is not welcome.
We also recommend that you read discourse’s rules for further suggestions on appropriate behavior.

title: PhD Presentations
text: Characteristics of Economic Time Series

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Volatility
Volatility means the conditional standard deviation of the underlying price return.






Implied they say!


The volatility value that, when plugged into a theoretical pricing model, reproduces the observed market price of an asset is termed as implied volatility

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Characteristics of Volatility
Unobservability

Volatility is not directly observable — only one return per trading day exists
Implied volatility is model-dependent and often overstates true volatility

Volatility Clustering

High volatility periods and low volatility periods tend to cluster together

Continuous Evolution

Volatility changes smoothly over time — abrupt jumps are rare

Mean Reversion (Stationarity)

Volatility does not diverge to \(\infty\) — it fluctuates within a bounded range

Leverage Effect

Volatility reacts asymmetrically to price increases vs. price drops

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Why Volatility?
The price level reveals the market’s current valuation of a commodity, while volatility captures the uncertainty and risk embedded in the price discovery process that led to that equilibrium.

title: PhD Presentations
text: Homoscedastic vs Heteroscedastic Process

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Problem

Many economic time series exhibit periods of unusually large volatility followed by periods of relative tranquility.
In such circumstances, the assumption of a constant variance (homoscedasticity) is inappropriate.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Model
One approach to model this behaviour is to explicitly introduce an independent variable \(x_i\) such that, \[
y_{t+1} = \epsilon_{t+1}x_t
\tag{1}\] where,
\(y_{t+1}\) is the dependent variable of interest;
\(\epsilon_{t+1}\) is a white-noise disturbance term with variance \(\sigma^2\);
\(x_t\) is an independent variable that can be observed at period \(t\).
Here, \[
var(y_{t+1}|x_t) = x_t^2\sigma^2
\tag{2}\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Logarithmic Form
Modifying the Equation 1, we get, \[
\ln(y_t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \ln(x_{t-1}) + e_t
\tag{3}\] where, \(e_t=\ln(\epsilon_t)\)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Regression and its Limitations
Since Equation 3 is linear, OLS can be used to estimate the parameters \(\alpha_0\) and \(\alpha_1\).
But,

The model forces \({x_t}\) to affect mean of \(\ln(y_t)\):
    It is difficult to choose one candidate for \({x_t}\) over many reasonable choices.
Transformations are required for the series to have a constant variance.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The “Conditional” Advantage
Consider a stationary ARMA model, \[y_t = a_0 + a_1y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t\]



Expectation Type
Mean
Forecast Error Variance




Conditional
\(E_t y_{t+1} = a_0 + a_1 y_t\)
\(E_t[(y_{t+1} - a_0 - a_1 y_t)^2] = E_t\epsilon_{t+1}^2 = \sigma^2\)


Unconditional
\(Ey_{t+1} = \dfrac{a_0}{1-a_1}\)
\(E\left[\left(y_{t+1} - \dfrac{a_0}{1-a_1}\right)^2\right] = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{1-a_1^2}\)



The conditional forecast uses information from the past and its variance is certainly less than the unconditional counterpart. Thus conditional forecasts are preferable.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Modelling Non-constant variance
When the conditional variance is not constant, a simple strategy is to model the conditional variance as an AR(\(q\)) process using squares of the estimated residuals: \[\hat{\epsilon}_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\hat{\epsilon}_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_2\hat{\epsilon}_{t-2}^2 + \cdots + \alpha_q\hat{\epsilon}_{t-q}^2 + v_t \tag{4}\] where \(v_t\) is a white-noise process.
If the values of \(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, ..., \alpha_n\) all equal to zero, the estimated variance is simply the constant \(\alpha_0\).






ARCH ALERT


The model in Equation 4 is the stepping stone to the ARCH process

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Flaw with additive model

In Equation 4, \(\hat{\epsilon}_{t}^2\) should be strictly non-negative. Also the coefficients are constrained to be non-negative to ensure the expected variance stays positive.
However, \(\nu_t\) is a white-noise process, it can take negative values. If there is a sufficiently large negative shock on \(\nu_t\), the entire right hand side of Equation 4 can become mathematically negative.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Multiplicative Alternative
In 1982, Robert. F. Engle proposed a class of multiplicative models as: \[\epsilon_t = v_t \sqrt{\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2} \tag{5}\] which is known to be the ARCH(\(q\)) process with \(\alpha_0 > 0\), \(\alpha_i \geq 0\) and \(\sum\alpha_i < 1\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Deriving the Conditional Variance
Starting from Equation 5:
\[\epsilon_t = v_t\sqrt{\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2}\]
Taking \(\text{Var}(\cdot \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\) on both sides:
\[\text{Var}(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})
= \left(\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2\right)
\cdot \underbrace{\text{Var}(v_t)}_{ = 1}\]
\[\therefore \quad \boxed{\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2} \tag{6}\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Compact Form of ARCH(\(q\))
Substituting \(\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2\) back into Equation 5:
\[\epsilon_t = v_t\sqrt{\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2}
= v_t\sqrt{\sigma_t^2} = v_t\sigma_t \tag{7}\]
where \(\sigma_t > 0\), since \(\alpha_0 > 0\) and \(\alpha_i \geq 0\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Unconditional Variance of ARCH(\(q\))
Since \(E(\epsilon_t) = 0\), we have \(\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = E(\epsilon_t^2)\).
Applying the Law of Iterated Expectations:
\[\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = E\left[E(\epsilon_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\right] = E(\sigma_t^2) = E\left(\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2\right)\]
Under stationarity, \(E(\epsilon_t^2) = E(\epsilon_{t-i}^2) = \text{Var}(\epsilon_t)\) for all \(i\).
Denoting \(\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = \sigma^2\):
\[\sigma^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \sigma^2 \implies \sigma^2\left(1 - \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i\right) = \alpha_0\]
\[\boxed{\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\alpha_0}{1 - \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i}} \tag{8}\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Mean of the ARCH(\(q\)) Process
From Equation 7, \(\epsilon_t = v_t\sigma_t\), where \(\sigma_t\) is \(\mathcal{F}_{t-1}\)-measurable.
Conditional Mean: \[E(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \sigma_t \cdot E(v_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \sigma_t \cdot 0 = 0\]
since \(v_t \sim \text{iid}(0,1)\) is independent of \(\mathcal{F}_{t-1}\).
Unconditional Mean: \[E(\epsilon_t) = E\left[E(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\right] = E[0] = 0\]
by the Law of Iterated Expectations.

title: PhD Presentations
section: Heavy Tails of the ARCH Process
text:
Heavy Tails of the ARCH Process
As long as \(\alpha_i \neq 0\) for at least one \(i\), \(\kappa > 3\), the ARCH(\(q\)) process has
heavier tails than the normal distribution, provided \(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^q 3\alpha_i^2 < 1\) where \(\kappa\) is the measure of kurtosis.

title: PhD Presentations
section: Testing for ARCH Effects: Engle’s LM Test
text:
Testing for ARCH Effects: Engle’s LM Test
\(\mathbf{H_0}: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \cdots = \alpha_q = 0\) (no ARCH effects)
Step 1: Fit an appropriate mean equation (ARMA or OLS regression) and obtain squared residuals \(\{\hat{\epsilon}_t^2\}\).
Step 2: Regress \(\hat{\epsilon}_t^2\) on its \(q\) lagged values as in Equation 4:
\[\hat{\epsilon}_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\hat{\epsilon}_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_2\hat{\epsilon}_{t-2}^2 + \cdots + \alpha_q\hat{\epsilon}_{t-q}^2 + v_t\]
Step 3: Compute the test statistic. Under \(H_0\):
\[NR^2 \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2(q)\] For small samples, an \(F\)-test with \((q,\ N-q)\) df is preferred.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Maximum Likelihood Estimation of ARCH(\(q\))
Given \(N\) observations, the parameter vector is \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_q)^\top\).
Conditional Log-Likelihood:
Assuming \(v_t \sim N(0,1)\), the conditional density of \(\epsilon_t\) given \(\mathcal{F}_{t-1}\) is:
\[f(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_t^2}}\exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon_t^2}{2\sigma_t^2}\right)\]
The conditional log-likelihood over \(t = q+1, \ldots, N\) is:
\[\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=q+1}^{N}\left[\log(2\pi) + \log(\sigma_t^2) + \frac{\epsilon_t^2}{\sigma_t^2}\right]\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
ML Algorithm

Initialize \(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(0)}\) — typically via OLS on \(\hat{\epsilon}_t^2\) against its lags
At iteration \(k\), evaluate \(\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2\) recursively for \(t = q+1, \ldots, N\)
Compute \(\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)})\)
Update \(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k+1)}\) via BFGS quasi-Newton optimization
Repeat steps 2–4 until \(\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k+1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\| < \delta\)







Constraints


Optimization is subject to \(\alpha_0 > 0\), \(\alpha_i \geq 0\), and \(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i < 1\) to ensure positivity of \(\sigma_t^2\) and second-order stationarity.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Effect of ARCH Coefficients


  
    
      α1 + α2 =
      0.05
    
    
    
      α1=—,
      α2=—
    
  

  
    
    
  










Alpha dynamics


As \(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1\), volatility clusters intensify and tails grow heavier — the ARCH process departs increasingly from normality.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Building an ARCH Model

Compute Log Returns — Transform raw price series \(P_t\) to log returns \(r_t = \ln(P_t/P_{t-1})\). This ensures stationarity in mean, removes trend, and stabilises scale.
Fit a Mean Equation — Fit an appropriate ARMA(\(p\),\(q\)) model on \(r_t\) using AIC/BIC. Extract residuals \(\hat{\epsilon}_t\).
Test for ARCH Effects — Run Engle’s LM test on \(\hat{\epsilon}_t\). Reject \(H_0\) to confirm volatility clustering warrants ARCH modelling.
Identify Order \(q\) — Examine PACF of \(\hat{\epsilon}_t^2\). The lag at which PACF cuts off suggests candidate \(q\).
Estimate ARCH(\(q\)) — Maximise conditional log-likelihood via BFGS. Verify \(\alpha_0 > 0\), \(\alpha_i \geq 0\), and \(\sum\alpha_i < 1\).
Diagnose Residuals — Standardise residuals as \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t = \hat{\epsilon}_t/\hat{\sigma}_t\). Check:

ACF of \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t\) and \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2\) for remaining structure
Ljung-Box test on \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2\)
QQ-plot for distributional assumption

Iterate if Needed — If \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2\) still shows significant ACF, increase \(q\) or consider GARCH.
Forecast — Use fitted \(\hat{\sigma}_t^2\) for volatility forecasting and interval construction.

title: PhD Presentations
section: Data: Deutsch Mark / British Pound Exchange Rate Returns
text:
Data: Deutsch Mark / British Pound Exchange Rate Returns







Data


Deutsch Mark / British Pound exchange rate returns.
\(N = 1974\) observations. Canonical dataset for ARCH demonstration.

title: PhD Presentations
text:

library(rugarch)
data("dmbp", package = "rugarch")
r <- dmbp[, 1]
head(r)

Step 2: Stationarity Test

library(tseries)

Dickey-Fuller = -12.268, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.01

Series is stationary — no differencing required

text:
Step 3: Fit Candidate Mean Equations

models <- list(
  "AR(1)"     = arima(r, order = c(1,0,0)),

ic_tbl <- data.frame(

ic_tbl

title: PhD Presentations
section: Step 4: ACF and PACF of Squared Residuals
text:
Step 4: ACF and PACF of Squared Residuals

acf_r2  <- acf(resid^2,  lag.max = 12, plot = FALSE)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 5: Engle’s LM Test

library(FinTS)
ArchTest(resid, lags = max_lag)




    ARCH LM-test; Null hypothesis: no ARCH effects

data:  resid
Chi-squared = 183.61, df = 5, p-value < 2.2e-16



\(p\)-value \(< 0.05\) — formally confirms ARCH effects
Maximum lag set to 5 from PACF inspection

title: PhD Presentations
section: Step 6: Fit and Compare ARCH Orders
text:
Step 6: Fit and Compare ARCH Orders

arch_fit <- function(q) {
  spec <- ugarchspec(
    variance.model     = list(model      = "sGARCH",
                               garchOrder = c(q, 0)),
    mean.model         = list(armaOrder    = c(p_order, q_order),
                               include.mean = TRUE),
    distribution.model = "norm"
  )
  ugarchfit(spec, data = r, solver = "hybrid")
}

q_max  <- max(max_lag, 2)
fits   <- lapply(1:q_max, arch_fit)
ic_mat <- sapply(fits, infocriteria)

ic_tbl <- data.frame(
  Order = paste0("ARCH(", 1:q_max, ")"),
  AIC   = round(ic_mat[1, ], 5),
  BIC   = round(ic_mat[2, ], 5)
)
ic_tbl

text:
Step 7: Best Model Estimates

coef(best_fit)



      mu      ma1    omega   alpha1   alpha2   alpha3   alpha4   alpha5 
-0.00110  0.04105  0.07880  0.24684  0.14579  0.09226  0.08572  0.12329

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 8: Residual Diagnostics

std_resid <- residuals(best_fit, standardize = TRUE)
ArchTest(std_resid,   lags = best_q)
Box.test(std_resid,   lag  = best_q, type = "Ljung-Box")
Box.test(std_resid^2, lag  = best_q, type = "Ljung-Box")




    ARCH LM-test; Null hypothesis: no ARCH effects

data:  std_resid
Chi-squared = 2.599, df = 5, p-value = 0.7615



    Box-Ljung test

data:  std_resid
X-squared = 3.5937, df = 5, p-value = 0.6093

data:  std_resid^2
X-squared = 2.5756, df = 5, p-value = 0.7651



LM test on \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t\): \(p > 0.05\) — no remaining ARCH effects
Ljung-Box on \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t\): \(p > 0.05\) — no linear autocorrelation
Ljung-Box on \(\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2\): \(p > 0.05\) — white noise confirmed

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 8: QQ-Plot

ggplot(data.frame(r = as.numeric(std_resid)),
       aes(sample = r)) +
  stat_qq(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "QQ-Plot: Standardised Residuals") +
  theme_classic()



Mild tail departure — Student-\(t\) innovations may improve fit

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 9: Volatility Forecast

forc <- ugarchforecast(best_fit, n.ahead = 20)
plot(forc, which = 3)



Forecast converges toward unconditional volatility
Confirms mean-reverting nature of the fitted ARCH process

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Motivation
Risk-averse agents demand compensation for holding a volatile asset — the risk premium itself should rise with conditional variance.
The Three-Equation System (Engle, Lilien & Robins, 1987):
\[y_t = \mu_t + \varepsilon_t \qquad \text{(Mean equation)} \tag{9}\]
\[\mu_t = \beta + \delta h_t, \quad \delta > 0 \qquad \text{(Risk premium)} \tag{10}\]
\[h_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^2 \qquad \text{(Variance equation)} \tag{11}\]
where \(y_t\) is the excess return, \(\mu_t\) is the time-varying risk premium, and \(h_t\) is the ARCH(\(q\)) conditional variance.






Key Interpretations



\(\delta > 0\) — higher conditional variance raises expected return (risk–return tradeoff)

\(\delta = 0\) — ARCH-M degenerates to standard ARCH; risk premium becomes constant \(\beta\)

\(\delta\) is estimated jointly with \(\alpha_i\) via MLE — its significance is the central test

title: PhD Presentations
text:
What is Symbolic Regression?
Symbolic Regression is a type of regression analysis that:

Searches the space of mathematical expressions to find a symbolic function that best fits a given dataset.
Optimizes simultaneously over the structure of the expression and the values of its parameters.
Operates without assuming a fixed model class in advance.

title: PhD Presentations
text: Modeling Approach

title: PhD Presentations
text: Types of Simulation

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Midsquare Method
This is the earliest arithmetic random number generator, proposed by von Neumann and Metropolis in the 1940s. It produces a sequence of pseudo-random integers by repeatedly squaring a seed value and extracting the middle digits.
Procedure:



Input: A four-digit seed integer \(Z_0\)




Square and pad: Compute \(Z_0^2\), padding with leading zeros to exactly 8 digits
\[Z_0^2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{pad}}\; \underbrace{d_1 d_2}_{}\underbrace{\boxed{d_3 d_4 d_5 d_6}}_{\text{middle 4}}\underbrace{d_7 d_8}_{}\]
Extract: Take the middle 4 digits \(\Rightarrow Z_1\)
Normalise: Set \(\;U_1 = \dfrac{Z_1}{10000} \in (0,1)\)
Iterate: For \(i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\)

\[Z_i \;=\; \text{middle four digits of } Z_{i-1}^{\,2}\]
\[U_i \;=\; \frac{Z_i}{10000}\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Middle Square Method — Procedure



Input: A four-digit seed integer \(Z_0\)




Square and pad: Compute \(Z_0^2\), padding with leading zeros to exactly 8 digits
\[Z_0^2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{pad}}\; \underbrace{d_1 d_2}_{}\underbrace{\boxed{d_3 d_4 d_5 d_6}}_{\text{middle 4}}\underbrace{d_7 d_8}_{}\]
Extract: Take the middle 4 digits \(\Rightarrow Z_1\)
Normalise: Set \(\;U_1 = \dfrac{Z_1}{10000} \in (0,1)\)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Linear Congruential Generator (LCG)
Introduced by Lehmer (1951), LCGs are the most widely used class of pseudo-random number generators. They produce a sequence of integers via a simple linear recursion modulo a large integer \(m\).
Procedure:



Input: Seed \(Z_0\), multiplier \(a\), increment \(c\), modulus \(m\)



The recursive formula is:

\[Z_i = (a \cdot Z_{i-1} + c) \pmod{m}\]

The \(U(0,1)\) output is \(U_i = Z_i / m \in [0,1)\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
LCG-Properties
The looping behavior of the LCG is inevitable. Whenever \(Z_i\) takes on a value it has had previously, exactly the same sequence of values is generated, and this cycle repeats itself endlessly.

The length of such cycle is called period of a generator. For LCGs, the maximum attainable period is at most \(m\). If the period of a generator is \(m\), then the generator is said to have full period

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Period of a Generator
The looping behavior of the LCG is inevitable. Whenever \(Z_i\) takes on a value it has had previously, exactly the same sequence of values is generated, and this cycle repeats itself endlessly.

The length of such cycle is called period of a generator. For LCGs, the maximum attainable period is at most \(m\). If the period of a generator is \(m\), then the generator is said to have full period

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Choosing LCG with full period

Hull-Dobell Theorem (1962)
The LCG has full period if and only if the following three conditions hold:

\(\gcd(c, m) = 1\)
\(p \mid m \implies p \mid (a - 1) \quad \text{for all prime } p\)
\(4 \mid m \implies 4 \mid (a - 1)\)


Choose a higher value of \(m \ge 10^9\)

title: PhD Presentations
text: Types of Simulation Models

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Variants of the LCG
The general Linear Congruential Generator recursion is:

\[Z_i = (a \cdot Z_{i-1} + c) \pmod{m}\]

This is called the Mixed Congruential Generator — it uses both a multiplier \(a\) and a non-zero increment \(c\).
By restricting the parameters, two important special cases arise:



Multiplicative Congruential Generator


Set \(c = 0\): \[Z_i = a \cdot Z_{i-1} \pmod{m}\]

Simpler and faster; widely used in practice
Cannot achieve full period (since \(Z_i = 0\) is absorbing when \(c = 0\))
Seed \(Z_0 \neq 0\) is required







Additive Congruential Generator


Set \(a = 1\): \[Z_i = (Z_{i-1} + c) \pmod{m}\]

Equivalent to repeatedly adding a constant increment \(c\)
Poor statistical properties; rarely used alone

title: PhD Presentations
text:
What is Response Surface Methodology?
Response surface methodology (RSM) is a collection of statistical and mathematical techniques useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes.
In general, suppose the experimenter is concerned with a system involving a response \(y\) that depends on controllable input variables \(\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_k\). The true relationship is:
\[y = f(\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_k) + \varepsilon \tag{1}\]
where \(f\) is an unknown function and \(\varepsilon\) is a random error term with \(E(\varepsilon) = 0\) and \(\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2\).
Taking the expectation:
\[E(y) \equiv \eta = f(\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_k)\]
The surface represented by \(\eta = f(\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_k)\) is called the response surface.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Natural and Coded Variables
The natural variables \(\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_k\) are expressed in natural units of measurement (°C, psi, g/L, etc.).
In RSM, it is convenient to transform to coded variables \(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k\), defined to be dimensionless with mean zero:
\[x_i = \frac{\xi_i - \bar{\xi}_i}{\Delta\xi_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k\]
where \(\bar{\xi}_i\) is the center point and \(\Delta\xi_i\) is the step size in the \(i\)-th natural variable.
The response function in coded variables becomes:
\[\eta = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Approximating Response Functions
Since \(f\) is unknown, we approximate it with low-order polynomials.
First-Order Model (for \(k\) variables): \[\eta = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_i x_i \tag{2}\]
First-Order Model with Interaction (for \(k = 2\)): \[\eta = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_{12} x_1 x_2 \tag{3}\]
Second-Order Model (for \(k\) variables): \[\eta = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum\sum_{i < j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j \tag{4}\]
The second-order model is widely used because it is very flexible, easy to estimate via least squares, and there is considerable practical experience indicating that it works well for real response surface problems.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Sequential Nature of RSM
RSM is inherently sequential:
Phase 0 — Screening: Identify which factors truly influence the response. Use \(2^{k-p}\) fractional factorials or Plackett–Burman designs.
Phase I — First-Order Exploration: Fit a first-order model. If adequate, use the method of steepest ascent (or descent) to move toward the optimum region.
Phase II — Second-Order Modeling: When curvature becomes important, fit a second-order model using designs such as the Central Composite Design (CCD) or Box–Behnken design. Perform optimization via canonical or ridge analysis.






The RSM Philosophy


Never undertake a response surface analysis until a screening experiment has identified the important factors. Budget for all phases — screening, steepest ascent, and second-order optimization.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Objectives of RSM
Response surface methodology is useful in solving three broad categories of industrial problems:
Mapping: Approximate the response surface over a region of interest so that the experimenter can predict the response for any set of operating conditions within that region.
Optimization: Determine the conditions that optimize the response — finding the maximum yield, minimum defect rate, or the settings that bring a response closest to a target value.
Robustness: Select operating conditions so that the response is insensitive to hard-to-control noise variables. This connects RSM to the broader field of robust parameter design.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Linear Regression Model
The general linear regression model relates the response \(y\) to \(p\) regressors:
\[y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p + \varepsilon\]
In matrix form with \(N\) observations:
\[\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \tag{5}\]
where \(\mathbf{y}\) is \(N \times 1\), \(\mathbf{X}\) is \(N \times p'\) (with \(p' = p + 1\)), \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is \(p' \times 1\), and \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\) is \(N \times 1\).
The least squares estimator is: \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} \tag{6}\]
with the covariance matrix: \[\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\]

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Test for Significance of Regression:
\(H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_p = 0\)
\[F_0 = \frac{SS_{\text{Regression}} / p}{SS_{\text{Residual}} / (N - p - 1)} = \frac{MS_R}{MS_E}\]
Under \(H_0\), \(F_0 \sim F_{p,\, N-p-1}\).
Test on Individual Coefficients:
\[t_0 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\text{se}(\hat{\beta}_j)} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 C_{jj}}}\]
where \(C_{jj}\) is the \(j\)-th diagonal element of \((\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\).
Under \(H_0: \beta_j = 0\), \(\; t_0 \sim t_{N-p-1}\).

title: PhD Presentations
section: Model Adequacy: Residual Analysis and Lack of Fit
text:
Model Adequacy: Residual Analysis and Lack of Fit
Residuals: \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\)
Internally Studentized Residuals: \(r_i = \dfrac{e_i}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2(1 - h_{ii})}}\)
where \(h_{ii}\) is the \(i\)-th diagonal element of the hat matrix \(\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\).
Testing for Lack of Fit: Partition \(SS_E\) into pure error (\(SS_{PE}\)) and lack of fit (\(SS_{LOF}\)):
\[F_{\text{LOF}} = \frac{SS_{LOF} / (m - p - 1)}{SS_{PE} / (N - m)}\]
where \(m\) is the number of distinct design points.






Diagnostics Matter


A significant lack-of-fit test is a strong signal that the current model is inadequate and a higher-order model (or a transformation) should be considered.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Fitting a Second-Order Model
The second-order model for \(k\) factors is:
\[y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum\sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon\]
In matrix notation: \(\quad \hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}'\hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{x}'\hat{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{x}\)
where:

\(\hat{\mathbf{b}}\) is the \(k \times 1\) vector of estimated first-order coefficients
\(\hat{\mathbf{B}}\) is the \(k \times k\) symmetric matrix with \(\hat{\beta}_{ii}\) on the diagonal and \(\hat{\beta}_{ij}/2\) off-diagonal

The number of parameters is \(p = 1 + 2k + k(k-1)/2\), so at least this many distinct design points are needed with at least three levels per factor.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The \(2^k\) Factorial Design
In a \(2^k\) factorial design, each of \(k\) factors is investigated at two levels (coded as \(-1\) and \(+1\)). The design has \(2^k\) treatment combinations.
For the \(2^2\) design with factors \(A\) and \(B\):

The regression model is \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_{12} x_1 x_2 + \varepsilon\), and the \(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\) matrix is \(4\mathbf{I}_4\). Hence all regression coefficients have equal variance \(\sigma^2 / 4\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Properties of \(2^k\) Designs
The \(2^k\) design is D-optimal for fitting the first-order model (with or without interactions). This means:

It maximizes \(|\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}|\), minimizing the joint confidence region for all coefficients.
The variance of any coefficient is \(\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \sigma^2 / N\), where \(N\) is the total number of runs.
All regression coefficients are uncorrelated.

These properties make \(2^k\) designs the ideal building blocks for RSM — they are used directly in screening, and they form the factorial portion of the Central Composite Design.

title: PhD Presentations
section: Adding Center Points to the \(2^k\) Design
text:
Adding Center Points to the \(2^k\) Design
Adding \(n_c\) center runs (all factors at 0) to a \(2^k\) design enables a test for curvature without affecting the estimation of main effects or interactions.
The test statistic is:
\[F_0 = \frac{SS_{\text{curvature}}}{MS_E} = \frac{n_F\, n_c\, (\bar{y}_F - \bar{y}_C)^2 / (n_F + n_c)}{MS_E}\]
where \(\bar{y}_F\) is the average of the factorial runs and \(\bar{y}_C\) is the average of the center runs.
Under \(H_0\) (no pure quadratic curvature): \(F_0 \sim F_{1, \, N - p - 1}\).






Why Center Points?


A significant \(F_0\) signals that the first-order model is inadequate and quadratic terms are needed — this is the trigger for augmenting to a CCD.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The \(2^{k-p}\) Fractional Factorial
When \(k\) is large, a full \(2^k\) factorial is too expensive. A \(2^{k-p}\) fractional factorial runs only \(2^{k-p}\) treatment combinations — a \(1/2^p\) fraction of the full design.
Key concepts:

Defining relation: \(I = \text{generators}\) — specifies which effects are aliased.
Resolution: The minimum word length in the defining relation.

Resolution III: Main effects aliased with two-factor interactions.
Resolution IV: Main effects clear of two-factor interactions, but two-factor interactions aliased with each other.
Resolution V: Main effects and two-factor interactions estimable independently.

Sparsity of effects principle: Most systems are dominated by main effects and low-order interactions.

The factorial portion of a CCD must have resolution V or higher to independently estimate all linear and interaction terms.

The projection property of fractional factorials means that every \(2^{k-p}\) design contains complete factorials in certain subsets of the original factors — this is valuable for discovering which factors matter most.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Method of Steepest Ascent
When the first-order model \(\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \cdots + b_k x_k\) is adequate, the path of steepest ascent moves the experimenter toward improved response values.
The procedure maximizes \(\hat{y}\) subject to the constraint \(\sum_{i=1}^k x_i^2 = r^2\). Using Lagrange multipliers:
\[L = b_0 + \sum_{i=1}^k b_i x_i - \lambda\left(\sum_{i=1}^k x_i^2 - r^2\right)\]
Setting \(\partial L / \partial x_j = 0\):
\[x_j = \frac{b_j}{2\lambda} = \rho\, b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, k \tag{7}\]
The coordinates along the path are proportional to the regression coefficients. The direction matches the sign of each coefficient; the movement in \(x_j\) is proportional to \(|b_j|\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Practical Steps in Steepest Ascent

Fit a first-order model using a \(2^k\) (or \(2^{k-p}\)) design with center runs.
Compute the path of steepest ascent from Equation 7 — choose a base step size in the most important variable and scale the others proportionally.
Conduct experimental runs along the path. Improvement should be observed initially but will eventually diminish.
When two consecutive runs show declining response, select that region as the base for a new first-order experiment — a mid-course correction.
If curvature is detected (significant center-point test), augment the design with axial points to form a Central Composite Design and fit a second-order model.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
The Second-Order Response Surface
The fitted second-order model in matrix form is:
\[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}'\hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{x}'\hat{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{x} \tag{8}\]
where \(\hat{\mathbf{B}}\) is the \(k \times k\) matrix of estimated second-order coefficients.
Finding the Stationary Point: Differentiating Equation 8 with respect to \(\mathbf{x}\) and setting to zero:
\[\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \hat{\mathbf{b}} + 2\hat{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}\]
\[\therefore \quad \mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\hat{\mathbf{B}}^{-1}\hat{\mathbf{b}} \tag{9}\]
The predicted response at the stationary point is \(\hat{y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s'\hat{\mathbf{b}}\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Canonical Analysis
The nature of the stationary point is determined by the eigenvalues of \(\hat{\mathbf{B}}\).
The canonical form of the second-order model is obtained by the transformation \(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_s = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{w}\), where \(\mathbf{M}\) is the orthogonal matrix of eigenvectors:
\[\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \sum_{i=1}^k \lambda_i w_i^2 \tag{10}\]
where \(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_k\) are the eigenvalues of \(\hat{\mathbf{B}}\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Ridge Analysis
When the stationary point lies far from the design region, or the surface is a saddle, ridge analysis is more informative than the stationary point alone.
Ridge analysis maximizes (or minimizes) \(\hat{y}\) on spheres of increasing radius \(R\) from the design center:
\[\text{Maximize } \hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to } \mathbf{x}'\mathbf{x} = R^2\]
Using Lagrange multipliers:
\[(\hat{\mathbf{B}} - \mu\mathbf{I})\mathbf{x} = -\frac{1}{2}\hat{\mathbf{b}} \tag{11}\]
For each value of \(\mu\), solve for \(\mathbf{x}\) and find \(R = \|\mathbf{x}\|\) and \(\hat{y}\). This traces the ridge of maximum response as a function of distance from the origin.






Practical Benefit


Ridge analysis reveals the best achievable response at each distance, the factor coordinates needed to achieve it, and which variables are most influential — providing guidance for further experimentation.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Structure of the CCD
The Central Composite Design (CCD) was introduced by Box and Wilson (1951). It is the most popular class of second-order designs.
The CCD consists of three components:
1. Factorial portion: A \(2^k\) full factorial (or a resolution V fraction), providing \(F\) runs at the \((\pm 1, \pm 1, \ldots, \pm 1)\) corners.
2. Axial (star) points: \(2k\) points of the form \((\pm\alpha, 0, \ldots, 0),\; (0, \pm\alpha, \ldots, 0),\; \ldots,\; (0, 0, \ldots, \pm\alpha)\).
3. Center runs: \(n_c\) replicated runs at the origin \((0, 0, \ldots, 0)\).
Total runs: \(N = F + 2k + n_c\)
The sequential nature is key: the factorial portion may already exist from an earlier screening phase. Adding axial and center points augments it to a second-order design.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Roles of the Three Components
Each component of the CCD serves a distinct estimation purpose:
Factorial points (\(\pm 1\) corners): Contribute substantially to estimation of linear terms and two-factor interactions. They are variance-optimal for these terms, and they are the only points that estimate interaction effects.
Axial points (\(\pm\alpha\) along each axis): Contribute to the estimation of pure quadratic terms \(\beta_{ii}\). Without axial points, only the sum \(\sum \beta_{ii}\) can be estimated. They do not contribute to interaction estimation.
Center runs (origin): Provide an internal estimate of pure error and contribute toward estimation of quadratic terms. Crucial for prediction variance stability.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Choice of \(\alpha\): The Axial Distance
The value of \(\alpha\) ranges from \(1.0\) to \(\sqrt{k}\):

\(\alpha = 1\) — Face-Centered Cube (FCC): All points lie on the faces of the unit cube. Only three levels per factor. Design region is cuboidal.
\(\alpha = \sqrt[4]{F}\) — Rotatable CCD: Ensures constant prediction variance on spheres centered at the origin.
\(\alpha = \sqrt{k}\) — Spherical CCD: All non-center points lie on a common sphere. Recommended when the design region is spherical.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Rotatability of the CCD
A design is rotatable if the prediction variance \(\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x})]\) is constant at all points equidistant from the design center.
For a second-order model, rotatability requires:

All odd design moments through order four are zero.
The ratio of moments satisfies: \(\dfrac{[iiii]}{[iijj]} = 3, \quad i \neq j\)

For the CCD, condition 1 holds automatically (with a resolution V factorial). Condition 2 requires:
\[\frac{F + 2\alpha^4}{F} = 3 \implies \boxed{\alpha = \sqrt[4]{F}} \tag{12}\]






Practical Guidance


Exact rotatability is helpful but not essential. If the design region is spherical, using \(\alpha = \sqrt{k}\) with 3–5 center runs gives excellent prediction variance stability throughout the region, even if the design is only near-rotatable.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Scaled Prediction Variance
The Scaled Prediction Variance (SPV) is defined as:
\[\text{SPV}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{N\, \text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x})]}{\sigma^2} = N\, \mathbf{x}_{(m)}' (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{x}_{(m)}\]
This standardized quantity allows fair comparison across designs of different sizes. The SPV should ideally be small and relatively constant over the design region — a rotatable CCD with adequate center runs achieves this.
A CCD with too few center runs will have an imbalanced SPV: the variance at the center can be several times larger than at the perimeter. Adding 3–5 center runs remedies this substantially.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Orthogonal Blocking in the CCD
Many experiments cannot be completed in a single batch. The CCD naturally accommodates blocking:

Block 1: Factorial points + some center runs
Block 2: Axial points + some center runs

Conditions for orthogonal blocking:

Each block must be a first-order orthogonal design (first moments and mixed second moments are zero within each block).
For each design variable, the sum of squares contribution from each block is proportional to the block size.

For a \(k = 2\) rotatable CCD (\(\alpha = \sqrt{2}\)), any even split of center runs between the two blocks achieves orthogonal blocking. This ensures that the block effects do not bias the estimated response surface coefficients.






Sequential Blocking


This blocking structure aligns perfectly with sequential RSM: the factorial block (from Phase I screening) is run first, and the axial block is added later when curvature is detected.

title: PhD Presentations
section: Step 1: Problem Definition and Factor Selection
text:
Step 1: Problem Definition and Factor Selection
Before designing the CCD, the experimenter must:
Define the objective — Is the goal to maximize yield? Minimize defects? Hit a target value? Optimize multiple responses simultaneously?
Select factors — Through prior screening experiments (Phase 0), identify the \(k\) most important quantitative factors.
Choose factor ranges — Define the center point \(\bar{\xi}_i\) and step size \(\Delta\xi_i\) for each natural variable. The coded range \([-1, +1]\) should span a meaningful operating range, and \([-\alpha, +\alpha]\) should remain within the region of operability.
Determine \(\alpha\) and \(n_c\) — Choose \(\alpha\) based on rotatability (\(\alpha = \sqrt[4]{F}\)) or sphericity (\(\alpha = \sqrt{k}\)). Use 3–5 center runs for variance stability.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 2: Generating the Design
For \(k = 3\) factors with \(\alpha = 1.682\) (rotatable):

Factorial portion: \(2^3 = 8\) runs at corners \((\pm 1, \pm 1, \pm 1)\)
Axial points: \(2 \times 3 = 6\) runs along axes at distance \(\alpha\)
Center runs: \(n_c = 6\) (recommended)
Total: \(N = 8 + 6 + 6 = 20\) runs

In R, using the rsm package:
library(rsm)
ccd_design <- ccd(
  basis = 3,        # k = 3 factors
  n0   = c(4, 2),   # 4 center in factorial block, 2 in axial
  alpha = "rotatable",
  randomize = FALSE
)
Each run is decoded back to natural variables using \(\xi_i = \bar{\xi}_i + \Delta\xi_i \cdot x_i\).

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 3: Data Collection
Randomization: Run the experiment in random order to guard against lurking variables and time trends.
Blocking: If the experiment spans multiple days or batches, use orthogonal blocking. Assign factorial + center runs to Block 1, axial + center runs to Block 2.
Replication: Center runs serve as replicates. They provide pure error degrees of freedom and allow testing for lack of fit without replicating the full design.
Recording: Carefully record the response \(y\) at each design point along with any covariates or nuisance variables.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 4: Fitting the Second-Order Model
Once data are collected, fit the full second-order model:
\[y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^k \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum\sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon\]
In R:
library(rsm)
fit <- rsm(y ~ Block + SO(x1, x2, x3), data = ccd_data)
summary(fit)
The summary output provides:

Coefficient estimates and \(t\)-tests
ANOVA table with lack-of-fit test
Canonical analysis: stationary point, eigenvalues, eigenvectors
Nature of the stationary point (maximum, minimum, saddle)

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 5: Diagnostic Checking
Before interpreting results, check model adequacy:
Residual plots: Plot residuals vs. fitted values, vs. each factor, and in normal probability plots. Look for non-constant variance, outliers, or systematic patterns.
Lack-of-fit test: The pure error from center-run replicates tests whether the second-order model captures all systematic variation: \[F_{\text{LOF}} = \frac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}}\]
A non-significant \(F_{\text{LOF}}\) supports model adequacy.
\(R^2\) and Adjusted \(R^2\): Evaluate the proportion of variance explained. The adjusted \(R^2\) penalizes unnecessary terms.
Influence diagnostics: Check Cook’s distance and leverage values (\(h_{ii}\)) for influential points.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 6: Response Surface Analysis
Contour and Surface Plots: Visualize \(\hat{y}\) as a function of two factors at a time (holding others at center levels).
In R:
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
contour(fit, ~ x1 + x2 + x3,
        at = summary(fit)$canonical$xs)
Canonical Analysis: Examine eigenvalues from Equation 10.

If all eigenvalues are negative → maximum found. Report \(\mathbf{x}_s\) and \(\hat{y}_s\).
If mixed signs → saddle point. Use ridge analysis to find the best conditions within the design region.
If any eigenvalue is near zero → ridge system. Ridge analysis along the near-stationary direction.

Ridge Analysis: Trace the optimum response on spheres from the center — identify the best achievable response at each radius and the corresponding factor settings.

title: PhD Presentations
text:
Step 7: Optimization and Confirmation
Single-response optimization: Use the stationary point from canonical analysis, or the ridge analysis coordinates.
Multiple-response optimization: When several responses must be optimized simultaneously, use the desirability function approach:
\[D = \left(\prod_{i=1}^m d_i^{r_i}\right)^{1/\sum r_i}\]
where \(d_i\) is the individual desirability for the \(i\)-th response and \(r_i\) is its importance weight.
Confirmation runs: Always conduct confirmation experiments at the predicted optimum conditions. Compare observed responses to predicted values — if they agree, the optimization is successful. If they disagree, further experimentation is needed.






Important Caveat


Estimated optimum conditions are just that — estimates. The next experiment may find the optimum at slightly different coordinates. What often matters more than the exact optimum is the understanding of how factors influence the response.
